This is why you may experience delays with this payment method.    We strongly recommend providing a bank account number (IBAN) as a payout method instead for future expenses. This allows for automation and will greatly speed up the processing.  If you still wish to be paid via PayPal, please verify your account.  Thank you for your help. Best, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">KYC create an organisation</td><td valign="top"> Hi,  Thank you for submitting the invoice.  As this appears to be an invoice from a registered business, could you please create an organization profile for the business, if you are its legal representative, and resubmit the invoice through that profile?  Under Belgian AML and KYC regulations, we need to ensure that the profile holder matches the payee, especially for business payments.  Here is how to do this: <a href="https://documentation.opencollective.com/getting-started/creating-an-organization">https://documentation.opencollective.com/getting-started/creating-an-organization</a>  Please include all of the business’s legal details in the organization profile.  Thank you for your help!  Best, </td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Business different than the account holder</td><td valign="top">Hi,  Thank you for submitting the invoice.  Everything looks good overall. We just need a small clarification: it is not clear to us what the relationship is between the business named on the invoice and the bank account holder.  Could you please explain this for us, as we may need to clarify it with the tax office?  Thank you for your help!</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Grant request documents</td><td valign="top">Hi,   Thank you for submitting the grant request.   To enable us to review and process it, could you please attach the following supporting documents:    a copy of the organization’s legal bylaws (statuts)    a signed letter formally requesting the grant, setting out:    the purpose and objectives of the requested funding    how the funds will be used in alignment with the fund’s mission    the legal name and contact details of the nonprofit organization      a bank account holder statement in the name of the nonprofit    For more details, please see our guidelines here: <a href="https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses/request-a-grant-for-nonprofits-only">https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/how-it-works/expenses/request-a-grant-for-nonprofits-only</a>  Thank you in advance.  Best regards,</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">No fiscal deduction in the EU</td><td valign="top">Hi,   thank you for your support.   Unfortunately, individual donations in the EU are generally not fiscally deductible, as there is no single harmonized tax framework across EU countries.   Tax rules vary by country, so we recommend checking what applies in your country of residence.     Best regards,</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">Application rejection OCEF</td><td valign="top">Hi,     Thank you for your application to be fiscally hosted by Open Collective Europe, and for taking the time to share your project with us.   After careful review, we have decided not to move forward with your application. Based on the information provided, we do not think the project is the right fit for OCE’s hosting framework at this stage.     We appreciate your interest and wish you all the best with the development of your initiative.     Best regards,  The OCE Team</td></tr><tr><td valign="top">After the onboarding call OSE</td><td valign="top">Hi,     Thank you for taking the time to speak with me earlier. As promised, here is a little recap on what we discussed about Open Source Europe (OSE).      OSE is a Belgian non-profit organisation focused on supporting impactful initiatives.  You can find our guidelines here and our technical documentation here.   To summarise it, here are a few key points :   Fundraising: OSE enables groups to raise money from various donors, including individuals, companies, and foundations.   Grant Applications: Your group is eligible to apply for grants. If you're applying for a grant, please inform us in advance so we can coordinate with the grantor to provide the necessary information and sign the grant agreement.   Responsibility: The collective's administrators are solely responsible for the collective's activities and management.   Expense policy:  This procedure applies for processing expenses.  Bank Accounts: Your collective cannot have external bank accounts. All funds must be held in your Open Collective account to ensure an accurate representation of your budget and activities.    Overhead costs:      8% for online (non-tax-deductible) donations.  10% for wire transfers, including grants from foundations and tax-deductible contributions from the US.   Lastly, feel free to join our Discord community to stay connected, share your progress, and engage with the broader Open Collective community.      Looking forward to seeing your initiative grow! We just need a small clarification: it seems that the user submitting the request is different than the payee. Under KYC regulations, we cannot process that type of request.     Could you please provide a bank account in your name?  To edit the payout method, simply click on the edit icon, and select Add New Bank account.  Thank you for your help! Since we need this information in order to continue reviewing your application, we will close your application for now.     You are welcome to get back in touch at any time if you would like to resume the process.     We wish you all the best with your project.     Best regards, The OSE Team</td></tr></tbody></table>

#### 2. Gemini for tone-checking

{% hint style="danger" %}
Always provide context to Geminibefore copy/pasting answers. A kind tone is always **crucial** in our field of work.
{% endhint %}

Team members are encouraged to use **Gemini** to polish or tone-check responses before sending, especially in sensitive situations. The tone of Gemini must be friendly and community-driven.&#x20;

Typical use cases:

* Rewriting emails to sound more diplomatic
* Checking for polite phrasing in tense exchanges
* De-escalating tone when a project is defensive or frustrated

This is not about writing robotic replies, but ensuring that our tone remains **respectful, clear, and constructive**, regardless of the situation.&#x20;

***

### Escalation guidelines

Escalate to Jean-François or Ivan when:

* The project is threatening legal action
* A grant or funder relationship is at risk
* There is confusion about the applicable legal structure (Foundation vs ASBL)
* A collective repeatedly disregards platform rules or compliance terms

When escalating, include links to all relevant emails or tickets and note any previous decisions.

***

### Reminder

Good support is not just about knowledge — it’s about tone, consistency, and building trust over time.\
When in doubt, write less, breathe more, and check in with a colleague before replying.


# Operational Checklist – Mandatory Before Action

This checklist must be followed before:

* executing a payment
* confirming instructions to a collective
* sending a contract for signature
* responding to operational or financial questions

***

### 1. Financial Verification

Before any payment, financial confirmation or contract request:

* Amount is correct
* Recipient is correct and verified
* Payment details (IBAN / method) are correct
* Transaction is compliant with internal rules

***

### 2. Fees & Conditions

* Applicable fee has been checked
* Any exception (e.g. 0% fee) has been:
  * confirmed
  * correctly configured on the platform
  * No default setting conflicts with the agreement

***

### 3. Platform Configuration

Before giving instructions (e.g. “you can add 10k”):

* Collective settings have been verified
* Fees are correctly set
* No incorrect parameter will be applied

***

### 4. Context Check

* I understand the specific context of this collective
* I have reviewed any prior agreement or exception
* No assumption is made without verification

***

### 5. Communication Check

Before sending any message:

* Message is clear and structured
* Tone is kind, professional and respectful
* Message is not overly directive or abrupt
* Financial or operational implications are accurate

***

### 6. Risk Check

* Is this a high-value or sensitive situation?

If YES:

* I have validated with Director or CFO
* OR I have explicitly confirmed all critical elements

***

### 7. Final Review (Mandatory)

Before executing or sending:

* I have re-read the action or message
* All critical elements have been double-checked

***

### 8. Escalation Rule

If any doubt remains:

* Do not proceed
* Escalate to the Director or CFO

***

## <mark style="color:$danger;">Important Principle</mark>

Execution without verification creates risk.

Verification is part of the task, not an optional step.

{% hint style="warning" %}
Always ask AI to verify any emails, contracts or other documents before sending them. See more in our [ai usage policy](https://docs.opencollective.com/oc-europe-internal-doc/tools-and-platforms-used-at-oce/ai-usage-policy)
{% endhint %}

## Accountability Rule

If an error occurs that should have been caught by the Operational Checklist, it indicates that the checklist was not properly followed.

In such cases:

* the process must be reviewed
* expectations must be clarified

and corrective measures must be implemented.


# Application Review

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

This page outlines our internal process for reviewing new applications to be hosted by Open Collective Europe (OCE). It serves as a reference for the support and operations team.

***

### Overview

Applications are submitted via the OCE public form:\
👉 [Apply to Open Collective Europe](https://www.oceurope.org/apply)

Each application is reviewed to determine:

* Whether the initiative aligns with OCE’s mission and impact areas.
* Whether it should be hosted under the **Foundation** or the **ASBL** structure.
* Any potential compliance or operational risks.

***

### Review Process

#### 1. Initial Screening

* **Eligibility Check**: Ensure the initiative meets the criteria outlined in our [Eligibility Guidelines](https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/getting-started/how-to-apply/eligibility).
* **Application Completeness**: Verify that all required fields are filled, including:
  * Collective's location (must be based in Europe).
  * At least two administrators with real names.
  * Description of activities and alignment with OCE’s mission.

#### 2. Assessment

* **Mission Alignment**: Evaluate if the initiative's activities further one or more of OCE's mission impact areas.
* **Legal Structure Determination**: Decide whether the initiative should be hosted under the Foundation or the ASBL, based on factors such as:
  * Nature of activities.
  * Funding sources.
  * Operational needs.

#### 3. Communication

* **Approval**: If approved, notify the initiative and provide onboarding instructions.
* **Rejection**: If rejected, inform the initiative with reasons and guidance on potential adjustments for reapplication.

<figure><img src="/files/QK9S67Nls61L7qhheJye" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

***

### Important Links

* [How to Apply](https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/getting-started/how-to-apply)
* [Eligibility Guidelines](https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/getting-started/how-to-apply/eligibility)
* [Application Questions](https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope/getting-started/how-to-apply/application-questions)
* [OCE Public Application Form](https://www.oceurope.org/apply)

***

### Notes

* Applications are typically reviewed within two business days.
* All communications with applicants should be documented in Freshdesk.
* For complex cases or uncertainties, escalate to the executive director or legal advisor.


# No Politics on the Platform

### Context

As a fiscal host and public utility foundation under Belgian law, Open Collective Europe must ensure that all hosted initiatives operate within the legal limits of non-profit, non-partisan, and non-political activity.

While we support many causes that are social, cultural, or ecological in nature, we must remain neutral in the face of party politics and electoral campaigning.

This page outlines what is and isn’t allowed when it comes to political activity by hosted collectives.

***

### What is not allowed

* Supporting, promoting, or opposing any **political party or candidate**, explicitly or implicitly
* Fundraising for **electoral campaigns** or affiliated political movements
* Using platform funds to **influence legislation or public policy**
* Displaying partisan symbols or slogans in platform profiles, updates, or tiers

These rules apply across all communication channels:\
Collective pages, updates, tier descriptions, images, and external links.

***

### What is allowed

* Hosting collectives focused on:
  * Human rights
  * Environmental justice
  * Civic education
  * Open source tools for activism (within legal limits)
  * Nonpartisan community organizing
* Engaging in public debate as long as the initiative:
  * Does not endorse or target specific political parties
  * Remains focused on its **mission-driven goals** in the public interest

***

### Examples

✅ Allowed:

* A collective advocating for climate justice through workshops and educational materials
* A group documenting police violence without endorsing candidates

🚫 Not allowed:

* A collective raising money to support a political campaign or candidate
* A group using fiscal hosting to fund party-affiliated propaganda

***

### Internal enforcement

If a collective is found to be in violation:

1. We issue a warning and request immediate correction
2. If noncompliance continues, we may freeze the collective
3. Repeated or severe violations can lead to permanent de-hosting

***

### Questions

If you're unsure whether a collective or project falls within the rules, contact the executive director or raise it in `#ocef`.

You can also review the platform-wide policy:\
[Open Collective Content Guidelines](https://docs.opencollective.com/help/product/content-guidelines)


# The Onboarding Call

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

## The Onboarding Call

This page provides a flexible script and checklist for onboarding calls with collectives applying to be hosted by Open Collective Europe.

Each call is adapted to the collective's profile, but the objectives are always the same:

* Present who we are and how fiscal hosting works
* Understand the initiative’s needs
* Confirm mutual alignment before onboarding

***

### 1. Introduction

* Thank them for applying and introduce yourself and your role.
* Explain the goal of the call:

{% hint style="info" %}
"We’ll use this time to walk you through how OCE works, how hosting would look for your project, and make sure this is a good match on both sides."
{% endhint %}

* If it's a video call: encourage everyone to **turn on cameras** for better human connection.

***

### 2. Who we are

* OCE is a **Belgian public utility foundation**. We offer legal and financial hosting for initiatives that don’t have their own structure.
* We are the **fiscal host**, not the tech platform — the platform is provided by [Open Collective Inc.](https://opencollective.com), based in the U.S.
* We focus on supporting initiatives in Europe and internationally with:
  * Transparent fund management
  * Grant reception (including from U.S. funders)
  * Expense handling
  * Funder compliance

***

### 3. Platform demonstration (recommended)

It’s very useful to **share your screen** during the call and show relevant live examples of collectives.

#### For community memberships & events:

* [Alversjö](https://opencollective.com/alversjo) – A good example of:
  * Membership tiers
  * Events and ticketing
  * Use of “Projects” as sub-budgets for organizing specific activities

#### For technical / open source projects:

* [Manjaro](https://opencollective.com/manjaro) – Classic OSS model, public budget, and regular funding
* [SUCHO](https://opencollective.com/sucho) – Response initiative with media attention, structured fundraising

#### Crowdfunding campaigns:

If a collective wants to run a **crowdfunding campaign**, they can activate it directly from their page.\
Instructions are available in the Open Collective platform documentation:\
[How to activate crowdfunding on your Collective](https://docs.opencollective.com/help/collectives/events#standalone-page)

Also mention that “Projects” are available as **sub-budgets** within a collective.\
These allow teams to allocate funds to specific goals, events, or working groups — with their own expense flows and transparency.

***

### 4. Who they are

Ask the collective:

* What are you working on? What’s your mission?
* Where are you based? (important for jurisdiction)
* Who is involved?
* How are you currently organized?
* Have you raised or plan to raise money?
* What kind of funding or donors are you targeting?

→ These answers help determine the hosting structure: **Foundation** or **ASBL**.

***

### 5. What we offer

* Legal & financial structure
* Collective page on the Open Collective platform
* Tools to collect contributions (cards, PayPal, bank)
* Expense processing and documentation
* Optional access to U.S. tax-deductible giving (via Myriad USA / Every.org)
* Support with funder processes, reporting, and compliance

***

### 6. What we don’t offer

* Employment contracts (but we can recommend Smart)
* Payroll, HR, or insurance
* Negotiated fees (see below)
* Custom fiscal or legal engineering
* Individual business hosting

***

### 7. The hosting fee

* Our standard fee is **10%**, and **not negotiable**.
* The fee covers legal, financial, admin, risk management, platform fees, and support.
* Only exception: For very large foundation-backed projects, we may adapt it in agreement with the funder.
* Otherwise, we keep the same fair structure for all.

***

### 8. How onboarding works

If the collective is accepted:

* We send a **Service Agreement** to sign
* Once signed, we create the page on the platform
* They receive an onboarding guide and can start fundraising

***

### 9. Common questions

**Can we use our own bank account?**\
→ No. OCE receives and holds all funds centrally.

**Do donors get tax receipts?**\
→ Only for U.S.-based individual donors (via Every.org/Myriad). Not in the EU.

**Can we invoice as individuals or orgs?**\
→ Yes, as long as documentation is valid and matches local regulations.

**Can we negotiate the fee?**\
→ No, except for major projects with dedicated funder discussions.

***

### 10. After the call

* Send a follow-up email with:
  * Service agreement template (if ready)
  * Link to documentation: [OCE Docs](https://docs.opencollective.com/oceurope)
  * Summary of next steps
* Add call notes in internal Slack or Drive


# Freezing Collectives

This page outlines the internal procedure for freezing a collective hosted by Open Collective Europe — either temporarily or permanently — when significant issues arise.

Freezing a collective means blocking its ability to receive or spend funds while the situation is under review.

***

### When to freeze a collective

Freezing is a last-resort action and should only be taken when one or more of the following applies:

* Evidence of **fraud or misuse of funds**
* Repeated **non-compliance with OCE’s terms or grant conditions**
* **Hostile, discriminatory, or threatening behavior** from collective admins
* Operating in **breach of platform rules or legal obligations**
* Legal or reputational risk to OCE (e.g., links to extremist activity or misinformation)
* Serious **funding disputes** with external partners
* Ongoing **internal conflict** within the collective that blocks normal operations

***

### Who can initiate a freeze?

* Any OCE team member can propose freezing a collective.
* Final decision must be made by the **executive director**.
* Legal and financial ops should be consulted when necessary.

***

### What happens when a collective is frozen?

* The collective’s **balance is locked** (no new expenses or payouts allowed).
* The collective is **unlisted from the public platform view** (optional, depending on case).
* The collective admins are notified by email, with an explanation.
* Any **donors or funders** involved are informed, if relevant.

In some cases, additional steps may include:

* Revoking admin access to the platform
* Pausing new incoming contributions
* Escalating to legal advisors

***

### Communication

A clear and neutral message should be sent to the collective admins:

> “Your collective has been temporarily frozen due to \[reason]. No funds can be spent or distributed until the matter is resolved. Please reply to this message if you wish to clarify or resolve the situation. We will follow up within \[X] business days.”

All correspondence should be archived in Drive or Freshdesk, and key actions should be noted in GitBook if precedent-setting.

***

### Outcomes

Once the issue is reviewed, one of the following outcomes is applied:

* **Unfreeze** (issue resolved, collective resumes operations)
* **Offboarding** (collective removed from the platform, funds transferred if applicable)
* **Ongoing freeze** (pending legal process or investigation)
* **Closure without transfer** (in cases of fraud, misuse, or forfeiture)

***

### Documentation & transparency

* All freeze decisions should be documented in the internal GitBook risk log.
* Slack discussions should be summarized for auditability.
* If offboarding or closure occurs, update platform records and funder contacts as needed.


# Virtual Cards

This page outlines the internal policy of Open Collective Europe regarding the use of virtual cards.

### General rule

We **do not provide virtual cards to hosted collectives**.

The use of virtual cards is restricted to internal Foundation operations only.\
No exceptions are granted to external collectives under normal circumstances.

***

### Internal use of virtual cards

OCE uses virtual cards exclusively for internal recurring operational expenses such as:

* Web hosting (e.g. our website)
* SaaS subscriptions
* Admin and infrastructure tools

These cards are centrally managed and only accessible by authorized team members.

***

### Exception: Global Statelessness Fund

An exception is made for the **Global Statelessness Fund**, which is hosted directly by Open Collective Europe and considered part of our internal structure.

* A virtual card has been issued for this fund via **Wise**
* It is co-managed by **Sabine** and **Elif**
* They are responsible for:
  * Ensuring the card is used exclusively for the fund’s mission
  * Uploading receipts for all expenses on the fund’s Open Collective page
  * Keeping the fund’s budget documentation up to date

This arrangement is contractually defined and is **not replicable for other collectives**.

***

### Oversight

* Virtual card activity is subject to internal review
* Ivan oversees all virtual card usage and reconciliations
* Any exceptions or edge cases must be escalated for approval

Always confirm with the financial ops team before issuing or proposing a virtual card.


# Google Workspace & Drive Structure

## Google Workspace & Drive Structure

This page outlines how Open Collective Europe Foundation uses Google Workspace — in particular Google Drive — to organize its files across operations, collectives, compliance, and funders.

Access is granted on a need-to-know basis, and folders should remain clearly named and navigable by all internal collaborators.

***

### General principles

* Use shared drives and folders rather than personal documents.
* Keep naming consistent: `YYYY-MM_Name_Type` (e.g. `2024-03_Alversjo_GrantAgreement.pdf`)
* Avoid duplicate versions of the same document in multiple folders.
* If in doubt, ask before creating new top-level folders.

***

### Suggested folder structure

#### 📁 `OCE – Admin & Governance`

For internal operations of the Foundation.

* `Board meetings & governance`
* `Legal status & statutes`
* `Accounting & tax filings`
* `Banking & payment infrastructure`
* `HR & contracts (staff or freelancers)`

#### 📁 `OCE – Hosted Collectives`

One subfolder per collective or fund hosted by OCE.

* Example: `Alversjo`, `Global Statelessness Fund`, `SUCHO`
  * `Grant agreements`
  * `Reports & deliverables`
  * `Due diligence`
  * `Funder communication`
  * `Notes & screenshots (if needed)`

#### 📁 `OCE – Funders & Grantmaking`

* `Private foundations` (subfolders per institution: Ford, Shuttleworth…)
* `Public funders` (EU, national, municipal)
* `Incoming grant contracts`
* `Regranting programs & funds`
* `Templates for funder reports`

#### 📁 `OCE – Support & Helpdesk`

* `Freshdesk exports (PDF)`
* `Canned responses`
* `Internal handbooks`
* `Templates for email replies`
* `Feedback from collectives`

#### 📁 `OCE – Risk & Compliance`

* `Due diligence process`
* `Risk log & escalated cases`
* `Sanctions checks`
* `Legal correspondence`
* `Archived rejection letters`

#### 📁 `OCE – Communication & Docs`

* `Branding & logos`
* `Presentation decks`
* `Public-facing docs (PDF)`
* `GitBook drafts or backups`

***

### Naming conventions

* Always prefix with **year-month**: `2024-04_Project_DocumentName`
* Use hyphens or underscores consistently — don’t mix.
* If a document is final: append `FINAL` or `SIGNED`
* Never delete shared documents — archive instead if no longer active.

***

### Access management

* Core team should have view/edit rights to most folders.
* Sensitive subfolders (legal, HR, risk) are restricted to designated staff.
* To request access: email <oce@opencollective.com> or post in `#ocef`.

***

Need help finding a document or cleaning up Drive?\
Ask Ivan or Jean-François before creating or archiving anything at the top level.


# OCE – Periodic Cleanup

This page outlines the recurring internal maintenance tasks carried out at Open Collective Europe to ensure organizational clarity, compliance, and smooth collaboration across the team.

These reviews are typically performed monthly or quarterly, depending on availability and capacity.

***

### Drive Cleanup

* Review shared folders, especially `Hosted Collectives`, `Funders`, and `Admin`
* Archive folders for inactive or completed projects (e.g. closed grants older than 12 months)
* Remove duplicates and rename files using: `YYYY-MM_Project_DocumentType`
* Move outdated working files into an `Archive` subfolder
* Confirm that shared access is correct and up to date

***

### Freshdesk Review

* Identify any open or unresolved tickets marked “Resolved”
* Tag valuable tickets for documentation and training reference
* Remove or update outdated canned responses
* Ensure any recent policy or fee changes are reflected in templates

***

### Platform Check

* Spot-check collectives for:
  * Updated team members and mission statements
  * Valid and documented expenses
  * Accurate public presence
  * Inactive “Projects” that should be removed

***

### Slack & GitBook Review

* Clean up outdated pinned messages or threads in `#oce-internal`
* Update GitBook pages where:
  * Processes have changed
  * Support questions recur regularly
* Document key internal decisions and team practices

***

### Finance & Reporting

* Check for grants requiring interim or final reports
* Confirm that bank transfers (especially from Every.org or Myriad USA) are matched with receipts in Drive
* Identify unallocated incoming funds and escalate if needed
* Ensure proper tracking for year-end reporting

***

### Risk & Compliance

* Update the internal risk log with new flagged cases or changes
* Review flagged collectives for progress or follow-up needs
* Audit due diligence folders for completeness and updates

***

### Email Templates for Collective Outreach

Use these templates when conducting outreach during cleanup periods. Customize them as needed based on each collective’s situation.

***

#### 1. No Balance / Inactive Collective

**Subject**: Follow-up on your Open Collective Europe page

Hi \[Collective name],

We noticed that your Collective currently has no available balance.\
If you’re not planning to raise or spend funds in the near future, we suggest closing your page to help keep the platform current and focused.

If you have questions or would like to stay active, feel free to reach out.

All the best,\
The OCE Team

***

#### 2. Might Not Be in Compliance

**Subject**: Review of your Collective profile

Hi \[Collective name],

During our routine check, we noticed some parts of your public profile may be outdated or missing information.\
To stay in line with our transparency and hosting requirements, please review and update the following:

* \[List of items to update]

Let us know if you need help with anything.

Best,\
The OCE Team

***

#### 3. You Might Not Want to Be Here

**Subject**: Is your Collective still active?

Hi \[Collective name],

It looks like there hasn’t been much activity on your Collective lately.\
If fiscal hosting through Open Collective Europe no longer suits your needs, we’d be happy to help you close the page properly.

If you’d like to continue, we’re here to support you as well.

Warm regards,\
The OCE Team

***

#### 4. How We Can Help

**Subject**: Support from Open Collective Europe

Hi \[Collective name],

We know running a project can take time and energy. Here are some ways we can support you:

* Guidance on setting up donation tiers and campaigns
* Tools for submitting and tracking expenses
* Help with generating funder reports or annual summaries

If there’s something you need, just ask — we’re happy to help.

All the best,\
The OCE Team

***

#### 5. Improve Your Public Profile

**Subject**: A few ways to improve your Collective's visibility

Hi \[Collective name],

We’d like to suggest a few small updates to help your Collective look more engaging and clear to visitors:

* Review your mission and about section
* Add a logo or header image
* Consider activating the “Projects” feature for sub-budgets
* Share recent updates or success stories

These small changes can make a big difference in how donors and funders perceive your work.

Let us know if you’d like help with any of this.

Best,\
The OCE Team

***

#### 6. Collective Activity Review (Announcement)

**Subject**: Collective Activity Review — OCE Hosting

Dear hosted collective,

Since 2018, Open Collective Europe has supported over 400 initiatives focused on building resilient communities, open innovation, sustainable ecology, and more.

To continue offering responsive and reliable hosting, we will be conducting a review of our collectives in the coming weeks. This will help us identify:

* Inactive or non-compliant collectives
* Projects that may need additional support
* Opportunities to streamline our hosting structure

If your collective is flagged, we will reach out with a list of actions to take and ask for a response within 30 days.\
Lack of response may result in freezing or un-hosting depending on your collective's situation.

Some collectives may receive a message simply asking how we can better support you. These messages are optional to reply to and sent only to collectives in good standing.

If you don’t hear from us, you can assume that everything is in order.

We look forward to learning more about your work and identifying ways we can help you thrive. If you have any questions, contact us at <oce@opencollective.com>.

Warmly,\
The OCE Team


# Financial Operations – Introduction

This section outlines the internal processes used by Open Collective Europe Foundation to manage financial operations across collectives, partners, and funders.

As a fiscal host, OCE is responsible for safely and transparently receiving, managing, and disbursing funds on behalf of more than 500 hosted initiatives.\
This documentation is intended for team members involved in financial workflows and compliance monitoring.

***

### Sections in this chapter

#### 1. Financial Ops Task Overview

A high-level summary of the recurring and ad hoc financial tasks handled by the team, including scheduling, responsibilities, and internal coordination.

#### 2. Bank Transfers for the Collective

Procedures for receiving and allocating manual bank transfers sent directly to OCE’s account. Includes how to match transfers to the correct collectives, and what to do when payment references are missing.

#### 3. Processing Expenses

How to handle submitted expenses: approval flow, supporting documents required, payout methods (Wise, PayPal), and timing expectations. Also includes how to handle edge cases (missing info, unusual currencies, etc.).

#### 4. Reconciliation Process

Step-by-step overview of how we reconcile platform data (Open Collective) with real bank activity. Covers matching transaction logs, resolving discrepancies, and updating internal records.

#### 5. Invoice and Corporate Donations

How to handle donations from companies that request invoices or proof of payment before contributing. This includes internal templates and who to loop in for confirmation and documentation.

#### 6. Refunds and Disputes

Covers cases where contributions need to be refunded, or where disputes arise (payment errors, double charges, fraud, etc.). Also includes the escalation process and coordination with OC Inc. or funders if needed.

#### 7. Budgeting and Forecasting

How we plan and track OCE’s internal budget. Includes tools used, funder projections, internal allocation of hosting fees, and how to support collectives in setting their own budgets.

#### 8. Platform Reconciliation with Bank Accounts

More technical procedures for reconciling the backend of the Open Collective platform with OCE's real bank accounts. Ensures our public-facing data matches accounting reality.

#### 9. Bank Accounts Management

Internal documentation on which bank accounts OCE uses, how access is managed, who initiates or approves transfers, and how Wise is integrated.

#### 10. Donations in Crypto Policy

How OCE handles cryptocurrency donations, via Every.org or other platforms. Covers which collectives are eligible, how crypto is converted, compliance concerns, and accounting treatment.

***

If you’re involved in OCE’s financial processes, this chapter is your reference.\
All new workflows or edge cases should be documented here to ensure traceability and consistency.


# Financial Ops Task Overview

This page summarizes the recurring financial operations managed by Open Collective Europe, organized by task type and frequency.

The purpose is to ensure smooth coordination, accountability, and documentation across the team — even as roles evolve or workloads shift. This is especially useful when onboarding new team members or during transitions.

***

### Weekly Tasks

These tasks are time-sensitive and must be performed regularly each week.

#### Mondays

* **Bank reconciliation and estimate — first round of the week**

  Match incoming bank transfers with the Open Collective platform. Read the Bank transfers for the Collectives chapter for details.

* During the reconciliation:

  * Identify unknown sources.
  * Tag transfers requiring follow-up.
  * Assign unidentified contributions to the holding fund, following the procedure for unclaimed or unreconciliable funds.
  * Estimate available funds and expected payout capacity for the week or upcoming period.

* **Top up Wise accounts**\
  Check Wise balances and request funds from KBC via Jean-François when necessary.

* **Check blocked or delayed expenses**

  Review expenses that are blocked, delayed, incomplete, or awaiting clarification.

  Follow up with collective admins or submitters where needed.

* **Check PayPal and Stripe disputes**

  Review whether there are any open disputes on PayPal or Stripe. Handle disputes according to the relevant dispute guidelines: confirm the transaction, gather evidence where appropriate, coordinate with OFiCo or OC Inc. if needed, and issue a refund only when required or approved.

* **Review incoming grant-related communications**

  Check for incoming grant-related emails, funder communications, due diligence requests, or banking documentation. Sync with JF and the grants team where needed.

* **Review Freshdesk tickets related to financial operations**

  Review and address Freshdesk tickets related to payments, expenses, bank transfers, refunds, grant payments, invoices, or other financial operations.

* **New collective onboarding — finance side**

  Handle finance-related onboarding steps for new collectives, including setup checks, payment configuration, banking information, and any related financial documentation.

* **Review incoming grant-related comms**\
  Check oce@ inbox for new grant agreements, funder updates, or due diligence requests.

***

#### Tuesdays

* **Expense payouts**\
  Process approved and ready-to-pay expenses through Wise, PayPal, or bank transfer.

  The payout order is:

  1. OCEF USD
  2. OCEF EUR
  3. OSE

  After payouts are processed, perform a second control round to check whether submitters have responded to incomplete expense requests.

  Follow up on incomplete, failed, blocked, or delayed expenses where needed..

#### Wednesdays

* **Application review and collective exchanges**\
  Evaluate new applications and exchange with collectives about missing information, eligibility, onboarding steps, or financial setup.
* **Collective onboarding** \
  Support the onboarding of new collectives, especially where financial setup, missing information, or platform configuration needs to be clarified.
* **Freshdesk review** \
  Review Freshdesk tickets and respond to finance-related questions or route them to the appropriate team member when needed.

#### Thursdays

* **Expense payout day** \
  Process approved and ready-to-pay expenses through Wise, PayPal, or bank transfer. The payout order is:

1. OCEF USD
2. OCEF EUR
3. OSE\
   After payouts are processed, perform a second control round to check whether submitters have responded to incomplete expense requests.

* **Check incoming grants on bank accounts**\
  Review bank accounts for incoming grants and ensure that they are identified, documented, and prepared for correct allocation on the platform.
* **Process operational expenses** \
  Process OCE operational expenses as needed. OCE Operational expenses are mostly paid manually via KBC Budget EUR account. Platform fees to OFiTech can be paid via Wise transfer to OFiTech Wise account. \
  Pay particular attention to **Wise virtual card expenses**, as these must all be added manually to the platform.
* **Collective onboarding** \
  Support onboarding calls and follow-up with collectives, especially where financial setup, documentation, or payout readiness needs to be confirmed.

#### Fridays

* **Review urgent Freshdesk tickets and late payouts** \
  Review urgent finance-related Freshdesk tickets, unresolved payout issues, and any late payments that need attention before the weekend.
* **Reconciliation of transactions in Zoho/Odoo** \
  Reconcile transactions in Zoho/Odoo and verify that all bank and Wise transactions are reflected correctly on the Open Collective platform. \
  This includes checking that incoming transfers, outgoing payouts, operational expenses, and manual adjustments are properly mirrored across systems.

***

### Monthly Tasks

These tasks help maintain overall financial hygiene and ensure external visibility.

* **Platform–bank reconciliation summary**\
  Double-check totals between the Open Collective platform and actual accounts.\
  Note discrepancies for escalation.
* **Fund allocation follow-up**\
  Remind collectives about updating their budget if large grants or restricted-use funds were received.
* **GitHub Sponsors income update**

  Update GitHub Sponsors income together with Open Collective Support / OFiCo Operations, currently coordinated with Sudharaka. Ensure that the GitHub Sponsors report is received, shared, uploaded, and checked for accuracy. Currently only on OSE.
* **Invoice and corporate donation requests**\
  Respond to companies requesting invoices or fiscal receipts for large gifts.
* **Internal budget review**\
  Track incoming fees, grants, and expenses for OCE's own operations.

***

### Quarterly Tasks

These tasks support external reporting, compliance, and strategic clarity.

* **Accounting and VAT closing**

  Coordinate with the accountants to close the quarter for VAT and verify their accounting records. Schedule calls with the accountants when needed to clarify discrepancies, missing documentation, or treatment of specific transactions.
* **Grant tracking review**\
  Update the list of active grants, deadlines, and reporting obligations.
* **Funder report prep**\
  Collaborate with the admin collective (or relevant lead) to generate financial reports as needed by funders.
* **Review crypto transactions and Every.org flows**\
  Ensure that all related donations are properly documented and reconciled.

  For ongoing Every.org fundraisers, check whether a payout should be requested and coordinate the follow-up when funds need to be transferred.

***

### As-Needed Tasks

These tasks occur irregularly but must be tracked and coordinated.

* **New collective onboarding (finance side)**\
  Set up Drive folder, check for service agreement, verify Stripe/PayPal/Wise linkage.
* **Large grant setup**\
  Ensure banking details and documentation are provided to the funder.\
  Confirm allocation of funds on the platform.
* **Refunds or payment disputes**\
  Issue refunds as requested on the platform, under **Transactions**, after verifying that the refund is required or approved.

  Coordinate with OFiCo when necessary.
* **Bank admin changes**\
  Manage access rights or initiate new banking connections (e.g., Wise admin handover).
* **Document exceptions and edge cases**

  Document exceptions or unusual cases in the appropriate place, depending on the situation:

  * **GitBook**, if the case creates a reusable process or precedent
  * **Drive**, within the relevant collective’s folder, if it concerns a specific collective
  * **Slack**, if the case requires short-term coordination or visibility
* **Flag blockers early**

  Flag blockers as early as possible to avoid delays, payment errors, reconciliation issues, or unclear responsibilities.

***

### Notes

* Ivan is lead on day-to-day financial ops, but other team members may step in during absences or workload surges.
* When documenting exceptions or edge cases, write them up in GitBook or leave a trace in Slack + Drive.
* Transparency is key — flag blockers early to avoid delays or errors.


# Bank Transfers for the Collectives

This page outlines how we manage incoming bank transfers on behalf of collectives hosted by Open Collective Europe. These may include donations, grants, sponsorships, or payments for services.

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

### General principles

* All funds received into OCE’s bank accounts are centralized.
* Collectives do **not** have separate accounts.
* Transfers must be matched manually and allocated correctly in the platform.
* Contributions made through Stripe and PayPal are automated. Manual wire transfers received directly into OCE bank accounts must be matched with the correct beneficiaries. Currently, this matching is done through **Zoho Books**, but the goal is to transfer this process entirely to the platform’s **Ledger → Bank Sync** tools. On OCEF, manual contributions mainly arrive in two bank accounts:

  * KBC Projects EUR
  * KBC Projects USD

  These accounts are operationally connected to the EUR and USD hosts. However, donations for a USD collective may sometimes arrive in the EUR account, and vice versa. In those cases, a currency exchange is necessary. For important amounts, use the **KBC spot exchange** option by asking Jean-François to process the exchange.

***

### How incoming transfers are identified

Each contribution has a unique **Donation Reference** **Number** generated when a contribution is initiated via the platform (e.g. manual bank contribution). It is a six digit number provided in the message with our bank account number they received.

We ask donors to include in the transfer reference:

* The **Collective name**, and/or
* The **Donation reference.**

This is critical to ensure that we can allocate the donation correctly.

***

### Common sources of bank transfers

* Manual donations via the “Bank transfer” option on the platform
* Large sponsorships or company donations initiated outside the platform
* Foundation grants wired after contract signature
* Transfers from Myriad USA (via Every.org)
* Transfers from private donors (who may not use correct references)

{% hint style="warning" %}
We receive a monthly payment from Stripe. There is no action needed as these funds are already recorded on the platform through the API.
{% endhint %}

***

### Weekly procedure

* **Incoming bank transactions are checked twice a week.**

  Currently, this is handled on **Mondays** and **Thursdays**.
* **Match transfers initiated through the platform** \
  The first step is to match transfers that were initiated through the platform and include a unique Donation Reference Number.

  To do this:

  1. Go to the Fiscal Host Dashboard.
  2. Open Ledger → Bank Account Sync.
  3. Use the Match function to match the bank transaction with the corresponding platform contribution. Use the screenshots below as visual guidance.

<figure><img src="/files/1xWoEL4Hmkgv6vq8YnBk" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/JpXqImAWDwonbTSELrK4" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/7N7e5GqH6fnOrIISxLjB" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

* **Allocate identifiable transfers without a Donation Reference Number**
  * Some funds arrive in the bank account without a Donation Reference Number.

    This often happens with grants, but the transfer may still be identifiable through other information, such as:

    * the name of the collective
    * the grant number
    * the donor or funder name
    * previous email correspondence
    * other payment references

    When the transfer can be identified but does not have a Donation Reference Number, allocate it using the **Add Funds** function in **Ledger → Bank Account Sync**, instead of the **Match** function.

    Use the screenshots below as visual guidance.

<figure><img src="/files/Mql99wka1EQUpIkXVIkz" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/JdnzTRUXMTMAm1qQCeIX" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/tFNeq5wXqm2cSvMXXO4S" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

* **Handle edge cases outside Bank Account Sync**

For the moment, there are still edge cases where the **Bank Account Sync** tool cannot easily be used, such as additional bank costs, multicurrency grants across hosts, cases where the amount received differs from the expected amount, cases where the received amount and bank charges need to be represented separately.

In these cases, use **Add Funds** from the **Action** button directly on the collective’s page.

This allows the platform record to accurately reflect the bank charges, the currency and the amount actually received.

When adding funds manually, include:

* the Donation Reference Number or other payment reference, if available
* a short internal note
* context if the transfer is external, grant-related, or unusual
* **IMPORTANT:** For grants added to collectives, do not apply the host fees - calculate the overhead ahead in base on the defined percentage with the collective, upload to the collective's balance the net amount, and add the bank charges. Overhead should be added as a separate contribution directly to OCE operations balance. \
  The tech team is working on finding a solution for this operation, similar to the host fees function.
* **Tag and monitor unknown sources**
  * If a transfer cannot be identified:

    * create a log in Drive or GitBook for unassigned funds
    * tag the transaction for follow-up
    * follow up by email when needed
    * ask the likely collective or donor to confirm the payment reference

    Unknown or unresolved transfers should continue to be monitored until they can be allocated or moved to the holding fund according to the relevant procedure.

***

### Unidentified transfers

If a bank transfer cannot be matched within a reasonable timeframe:

* Funds are placed in a **holding collective** (internal platform account)
* We try to identify the source via email records or Freshdesk
* If no match is found after **24 months**, funds may be reallocated to OCE’s general operations (see policy)

***

### Best practices

* Encourage collectives to instruct donors to always include a reference
* If a collective expects a major incoming payment, ask them to notify us in advance
* When allocating funds manually, double-check:
  * The correct currency
  * The collective's name spelling
  * The donor visibility setting (public vs anonymous)

***

### Notes

* Ivan currently leads this task, but any finance ops team member may be involved during absences or workload peaks.
* All fund allocations should be documented (Freshdesk ticket, internal note on platform, and Drive confirmation if large).


# Payment processing and disbursements

This page outlines the internal procedures for reviewing, approving, and paying expenses submitted by collectives hosted by Open Collective Europe (OCE).\
It is intended for internal use by the OCE finance team.

***

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

### Overview

Open Collective Europe processes three main types of disbursements:

* **Reimbursements**: submitted by individuals who paid out-of-pocket for a collective or fund.
* **Invoices**: submitted by external service providers, contractors, freelancers, or companies billing a collective or fund.
* **Grants**: submitted by individuals or organizations requesting grant funding from a collective or fund.

Invoices and reimbursements are submitted through the **Submit Expense** flow.

Grants are requested through the **Request Grant** flow.

All disbursement requests must be submitted through the Open Collective platform and follow a two-step procedure:

1. **Collective / fund admin approval**
2. **OCE review**

***

### Where disbursements are managed

Disbursements are managed through the Host Dashboard:

**Outgoing money > Pay disbursements**

In the **Outgoing money** section, you can also check previous disbursement requests and their status, such as:

* paid;
* incomplete;
* approved;
* rejected;
* ready to pay.

<figure><img src="/files/0CTmYeuLSczhKX1RHqMb" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

### Submission guidelines<br>

### Reimbursements

Reimbursements are for people who paid costs personally and are requesting repayment.&#x20;

* Must include a valid **receipt**, showing:
  * Vendor name and address
  * Transaction date
  * Description of what was purchased
  * Total amount paid
* Pro forma invoices or order confirmations are **not** accepted
* If only part of a receipt is being reimbursed:
  * Highlight clearly or
  * Include a signed explanation from the payee
* See the Quarterly Accounting page for Operational Reimbursement requirements

Pro forma invoices, quotes, order confirmations, shopping carts, or unpaid invoices are not accepted as proof of payment for reimbursements.

If only part of a receipt is being reimbursed, the submitter must either:

* clearly highlight the reimbursed items; or
* include a signed explanation from the payee.

#### Invoices

Invoices are used when an external provider, freelancer, contractor, or company bills the collective or fund for services.

An invoice must include:

* a unique invoice number;
* the provider’s legal name;&#x20;
* the provider’s address;&#x20;
* the provider’s tax ID, if applicable;&#x20;
* a clear description of the services provided;&#x20;
* the total amount;
* the tax rate, if applicable. If the tax rate is zero, there must be a legal reference for this (e.g., VAT exempt according to the VAT Law Y, article X).&#x20;
* the correct addressee: Collective, c/o Admin name and surname, Admin's adress. The invoice **must not** include any reference to "Open Collective Europe".

#### Grants

Grants are requested through the **Request Grant** flow.

Before processing a grant, check that:

* the grant is documented with: a registration document of the non-profit requesting it, a signed letter officialy requesting the grant, an official bank account holder statement. This serves to establish the purpose of the grant is clear; the recipient information is complete and the payout method is valid;
* the grant does not raise unresolved compliance, AML, KYC, or sanctions concerns.

***

### Approval Workflow

1. The submitter creates a disbursement request on the Open Collective platform.
2. The collective or fund admin reviews and approves the request.
3. The OCE finance team performs a final review before payment.

During the OCE review, check whether:

* the supporting documentation is complete;
* the disbursement type is correct (reimbursement, invoice, grant);
* the payout method is valid;&#x20;
* the payout details are accurate;&#x20;
* the request is eligible and compliant (clear connection with the activity of the collective, proper title);

If documentation is missing, incorrect, or unclear, mark the request as **Incomplete** and leave a clear comment explaining what needs to be corrected.

***

### AML / KYC checks

For AML and KYC reasons, it is important that the user who submitted the disbursement request and the actual payee are the same person or organization.

Because of this, it is mandatory to ask every user who has not done so to fill out the **Legal name** section of their user profile. Disbursement to a PayPal account can only be processed to verified accounts. &#x20;

The legal name should match the name of the person or organization receiving the funds.

This also means that if an organization is submitting an invoice to a collective, it should do it through an organization, and not an individual account.

{% hint style="warning" %}
Do not process a disbursement if the submitter and the payee do not match, unless the exception is clearly justified and documented.
{% endhint %}

In rare cases, such as some humanitarian grants, OCE may accept payment to an intermediary. This must be clearly motivated and documented in writing.

### Before Processing Payments

Before starting a payment batch, make sure that there are sufficient funds on the relevant Wise account of the host.

The Wise account of **OSE** receives contributions directly, so the budget is generally available.

The Wise accounts of **OCEF-EUR** and **OCEF-USD** must be recharged from:

* **KBC Projets Fondation - EUR**
* **KBC Projets Fondation - USD**

Take transfer timing into account (plan ahead!) to avoid unnecessary delays; in particular with the USD transfers.

#### Recharging Wise EUR

To recharge **Wise EUR**:

1. Log in to Wise.
2. Select the **EUR** account.
3. Click the **Add +** icon.
4. Select **External account**.
5. Transfer the funds from **KBC Projets Fondation - EUR**.

Amounts up to 20,000 EUR can be transferred from the KBC Projets Fondation - EUR account using the KBC Business Dashboard application. For amounts above 20,000 EUR, encode a wire transfer from the banking app. These transfers are immediate and free of charge.

<figure><img src="/files/IanRztUa6MPNt7hkAu2Z" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/ZbtQmAzNRrg4MQ3SJBf5" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

#### Recharging Wise USD

The process for **Wise USD** is different.

Transfers can **take up to a week to arrive** and incur bank charges on both the Wise and KBC sides. It is mandatory to go through the Add funds procedure on Wise, otherwise the arrival of the funds may be delayed or rejected.

To recharge **Wise USD**:

1. Log in to Wise.
2. Select the **USD** account.
3. Click the **Add +** icon.
4. Select **International Wire**.
5. Take Wise bank charges into account.
6. Prepare the wire from **KBC Projets Fondation - USD** to the account details provided by Wise.
7. In KBC, select **USD** as the currency.
8. Select the option **“Tout frais à charge de donneur d’ordre”**.

Wise charges **6.11 USD** regardless of the amount transferred. Add this amount to the total you are sending from KBC Brussels. For example, if you want that Wise receives exactly 50,000 USD, the wire amount from KBC should include the Wise fee: 50,000 USD + 6.11 USD = 50,006.11 USD

The bank charges incurred on KBC Brussels for this transfer are in percentage, and are included in OCE operational costs through the monthly invoice issued by KBC Brussels.

{% hint style="warning" %}
USD transfers to Wise are not immediate. They can take up to a week and include charges. Plan USD liquidity in advance.
{% endhint %}

<figure><img src="/files/CQuykYDG5rl6BZxeJOPZ" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/7OHulVBPuYke2Z3Pry3s" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/RBJTP9VgUa5aIPHJcxS0" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

<figure><img src="/files/jWkcWsNOkSIbT29EgiZ2" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

### Payment Processing

Payments are reviewed and processed **twice a week** (typically Tuesday and Thursday). This schedule may change depending on holidays, team availability, cash availability, urgent cases, or large payments that require direct processing from KBC. Most of the collectives are used to their disbursements being processed on Tuesdays.

#### Step-by-step process:

1. Go to the Host Dashboard: **Outgoing money > Pay disbursements.** The available balances are shown on the dashboard page. Recharge Wise if necessary. The disbursements with status **"Ready to Pay"** are autoamtically listed.&#x20;
2. Sort by date submitted and review each request individually. "Ready to Pay" means that the collective admin has approved the expense, and that collective has enough funds to pay it out. It **does not mean** that it contains all the elements we need!
3. **Verify each expense**
   * Check that documentation is correct and matches internal policies
   * Ensure payout details are accurate.

{% hint style="danger" %}
Confirm the name on the bank account matches the **submitter's legal name**

* This is especially important for bank transfers via Wise
  {% endhint %}

1. **Process the payout**
   * Use Wise for EUR and international bank transfers
   * Use PayPal if selected and valid. Process only the payments that include a **verified  PayPal account** (marked in green next to the payout method), AND the **Legal name** of the submitte&#x72;**.**
   * Use KBC Brussels to pay out special cases, such as GSF grants to countries not covered by Wise, or big invoices in USD (above 25k).
   * Record the transaction reference if not automatically updated by the platform
2. **Mark as paid**
   * Expenses are automatically marked as paid by the platform once the transaction is completed
   * For manual transfers, update the platform manually if necessary

{% hint style="warning" %}
Bear in mind that for the moment, due to the change in the **PayPal** procedure, all PayPal disbursements are **entirely manual**. You have to log on the PayPal account, pay it from there using the account the submitter provided, and mark it manually as Paid on the platform.&#x20;
{% endhint %}

#### Direct payments from KBC

In some cases, payments must be made directly from KBC Brussels accounts instead of Wise.

This can happen for example with GSF grants to countries Wise does not work with; large invoices in USD; urgent payments where transferring funds to Wise first would create unnecessary delays.

For example, under the current cash flow, it is much faster to pay out a **75,000 USD** invoice submitted to **AOS** directly from KBC than to transfer the funds to Wise and then pay it out from there.

Before paying directly from KBC, check:

* whether the payment cannot reasonably be made through Wise;
* whether the bank details are complete and verified;
* whether the supporting documentation is complete;
* whether the payment has been approved on the platform;
* whether the direct payment will need to be manually reflected or documented on the platform.

{% hint style="danger" %}
For wires in USD, the bank charge must be added to the disbursement in the processing fee section. It will take time before it is processed and recorded on the KBC dashboard. Wait until you have the bank charge before marking the disbursement as paid manually. &#x20;
{% endhint %}

<figure><img src="/files/00x0AJIS0OIPihHNCDPZ" alt=""><figcaption></figcaption></figure>

***

### Common Issues and Resolutions

| Issue                                              | What to do                                                                                                             |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Missing documents                                  | Mark the request as **Incomplete** and notify the submitter with a clear comment.                                      |
| Incorrect document                                 | Mark as **Incomplete** and explain what document is required.                                                          |
| Pro forma invoice submitted as reimbursement proof | Ask for proof of payment or a valid receipt.                                                                           |
| Wrong payout name or bank account mismatch         | Contact the submitter and request a correction before payment.                                                         |
| Legal name missing from user profile               | Ask the user to complete the **Legal name** section before processing.                                                 |
| Submitter and payee do not match                   | Do not process unless there is a clearly documented and justified exception.                                           |
| Payment fails on Wise or PayPal                    | Retry once. If it fails again, follow up and document the issue.                                                       |
| Duplicate submission                               | Approve only one request and reject the duplicate with a clear reason.                                                 |
| Insufficient Wise balance                          | Recharge Wise from the relevant KBC account before processing.                                                         |
| USD transfer to Wise would take too long           | Consider direct payment from KBC if appropriate and documented.                                                        |
| Unclear eligibility                                | Ask for an explanation of the expense to the collective. Flag internally in doubt.                                     |
| Unverified PayPal account                          | Ask the submitter to verify the account and fill out the Legal name section of their profile if it isn't already done. |

***

### Best Practices

* Process disbursements only after full verification.
* Always match payout details with the submitter’s identity.&#x20;
* Ask users to complete their legal name when missing. &#x20;
* Do not pay requests with incomplete or unclear documentation.
* Check Wise balances before starting payment processing.&#x20;
* Plan liquidity in advance, in particular in USD, because transfers to Wise can take up to a week.&#x20;
* Consider direct KBC payment for large or urgent payments in USD where Wise would create unnecessary delays.
* Flag unclear cases internally before processing.
* Always add clear, friendly and constructive comments when marking requests as incomplete or rejected.
* Avoid processing exceptions without documenting the reason.


# GitHub Sponsors Payments

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

This page documents how GitHub Sponsors payments are processed and allocated internally by Open Collective Europe Foundation.

These instructions are intended for internal use by the finance team and support staff.

***

### Context

GitHub Sponsors allows individuals and organizations to support open source projects directly on GitHub. Some of our hosted collectives use this feature to collect recurring or one-off donations.

Each month, GitHub aggregates sponsorship payments and transfers the total amount to OCE’s bank account.

***

### Internal process

1. **Monthly payment**\
   A monthly payment from GitHub Sponsors is sent to OSE's Wise account. \
   It is a batched payment for all the collectives receiving Github Sponsors.
2. **Monthly report**\
   A monthly GitHub Sponsors report can be downloaded from [this page](https://github.com/sponsors/hello-europe/dashboard/fiscal_host). \
   This report contains the list of sponsored projects and amounts. Without this report, we cannot accurately allocate funds.
3. **Action required**
   * Connect to [Github Sponsors dashboard](https://github.com/sponsors/hello-europe/dashboard/fiscal_host).\
     Under **Export payouts history**, choose a payout for the current month and click on "Export".  The report will be sent to <oce@opencollective.com>.&#x20;
   * Check <oce@opencollective.com> for the report (it will be seen in FreshDesk too).
   * Share the report with OFiCo Operations (Spring 2026 - Sudharaka is doing it) to upload them for us.
   * Verify the uploaded funds to check for accuracy.
4. **Funds reception**\
   GitHub transfers the total sponsorship amount to OCE’s bank account (typically mid-month for the previous month).
5. **Allocation**\
   Based on the report, OFiCo Operations:
   * Allocate each portion of the received funds to the appropriate collective via the platform.
   * As soon as the tool is developed for our team to do that, we will take over this task.
6. **Fees**\
   A standard **10% OCE hosting fee** applies before allocation.

***

### Notes

* There may be a delay between the bank transfer and the fund allocation due to reporting lag.
* Always keep a copy of the GitHub Sponsors report in the appropriate [Drive folder](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1xvOWbvDs9OF0XTmK5XOC6BORoyUhQL4W) once received from JF.
* Allocation must be done manually and transparently — include transaction notes and cross-check amounts.

***

### Contact

If anything is unclear or if the GitHub Sponsors report is missing, follow up with Jean-François directly or escalate in `#ocef`.


# Payroll Expense Processing

This page outlines how payroll-related expenses are handled internally at Open Collective Europe Foundation.

### Overview

OCE currently has staff employed under Belgian law. Payroll administration is handled through a registered social secretariat.

***

### Payroll provider

* Our payroll is managed via **SD Worx**, a certified **Belgian social secretariat**.
* All Belgian employees are registered through SD Worx.
* SD Worx provides monthly payslips, calculates payroll taxes, and manages official filings with the Belgian authorities.

***

### Roles and responsibilities

#### Jean-François

* Logs into the SD Worx portal each month to **submit salary data for processing**.
* Coordinates with SD Worx to ensure salaries are calculated and processed correctly.
* Does **not initiate payroll payments** — these are executed automatically by SD Worx once processed.

#### Ivan

* Responsible for **uploading payroll costs to the Open Collective platform** after salaries are paid.
* Tasks include:
  * Creating a single monthly expense entry (e.g. “March 2025 Payroll”)
  * Attaching the SD Worx summary as supporting documentation
  * Allocating the cost to the appropriate internal fund or project

***

### Notes

* Payroll costs are part of OCE’s internal operating budget and not visible to hosted collectives.
* Access to payroll-related documents is limited to Jean-François and Ivan.
* Expenses uploaded to the platform must avoid any personal or confidential data.

***

### Questions

* For questions about salary processing with SD Worx, contact Jean-François.
* For questions about expense entries on the platform, contact Ivan.


# Reconciliation Process

This page describes how we reconcile Open Collective platform data with our actual bank accounts.\
The goal is to ensure that funds received and spent are accurately reflected across our financial systems and meet audit expectations.

***

### Why reconciliation matters

* To confirm that all incoming and outgoing transactions are accounted for
* To detect errors, duplicates, or missing documentation
* To provide accurate reporting to funders and regulatory authorities
* To align platform records with real-world financial activity

***

### Scope

Reconciliation includes matching the following:

* **Bank accounts** (Wise, KBC Brussels, PayPal)
* **Transactions in the Open Collective platform**
* **Grant agreements, funder reports, or expense receipts in Drive**

This process applies to both internal OCE funds and hosted collectives.

***

### Frequency

* **Light check**: Weekly (informal, during platform usage)
* **Structured reconciliation**: Monthly (minimum), including:
  * Full review of incoming transfers
  * Random sample of expenses
  * Review of Every.org / Myriad flows

***

### Step-by-step procedure

#### 1. Collect sources

* Export transaction logs from:
  * Open Collective platform (Host Dashboard > Reports)
  * Wise (monthly export)
  * PayPal (monthly export)
  * KBC Brussels (PDF/CSV)
* Open your working Drive spreadsheet or create a new one in:\
  `Drive > OCE – Admin > Finance > Reconciliation`

***

#### 2. Match incoming transactions

* Check incoming bank transfers against:
  * Platform contributions
  * Manual "Add Funds" entries
  * Grants from funders (via reports or agreements)
* Flag any unmatched transfers in a “To Investigate” tab.

***

#### 3. Match outgoing transactions

* Check expenses paid (on the platform) vs:
  * Wise / PayPal payout logs
  * Receipts stored in Drive (for large or high-risk expenses)
  * Expense notes and platform audit trails
* Check if any “Ready to Pay” or “Incomplete” expenses remain unresolved

***

#### 4. Spot-check collectives

Choose 2–3 active collectives and verify:

* Most recent grant or donation received → matched in bank
* Most recent expense → receipt is valid and payout confirmed
* Admin notes (if needed) are present

***

#### 5. Flag discrepancies

* Unmatched transfers: escalate to Ivan or Jean-François
* Duplicate expenses: mark and reject in platform
* Missing receipts: notify submitter or collective admin

Document any issues in your monthly reconciliation spreadsheet.

***

### Tools

* Google Drive (shared reconciliation folder)
* Open Collective Host Reports
* Wise & PayPal dashboards
* KBC Brussels online banking export

If anything is unclear or missing, document it and ask for clarification in `#oce-internal`.


# Unclaimed or Unreconciliable Funds

This page outlines how Open Collective Europe manages funds that have been received into our accounts but cannot be clearly linked to a specific collective or donation.

These may include:

* Manual bank transfers with no reference or incomplete information
* Contributions from third-party fundraising platforms with no identifiable source
* Misrouted payments
* Funds from anonymous donors that remain unverified

***

### Process

#### 1. Initial attempt to identify the funds

* Check whether a matching order exists on the platform
* Look for any recent communication via Freshdesk or oce@ email
* Contact the likely collective (if suspected) to request confirmation
* Ask the donor for proof of payment if contact is known

#### 2. Temporary allocation

If no match is found after a reasonable first investigation:

* Funds are allocated to a **holding collective** (internal platform account for OCE)
* Document the transaction in the **“Unallocated Funds”** spreadsheet in Drive:
  * Date received
  * Amount
  * Currency
  * Payment source (Wise, PayPal, KBC)
  * Any known reference
  * Steps taken to investigate

***

### Retention period

Unclaimed or unreconciled funds are held for a period of **two years** (24 months) from the date of reception.

During this time:

* We continue to allow possible matching if the donor or collective contacts us
* Funds remain frozen and are not used

***

### After 24 months

If the funds remain unclaimed after 2 years:

* They are reallocated to the **Open Collective Europe General Operating Budget**
* This process must be recorded in:
  * The reconciliation log
  * The collective history field on the platform (for auditability)

***

### Notes

* No funds are ever redirected without a minimum documentation trail
* Collectives should be reminded to inform donors to include proper references in bank transfers
* If a collective disputes the reallocation after the 2-year period, escalation goes to the Executive Director

***

For questions, contact Ivan or raise the issue in `#ocef`.


# Refunds & Disputes

{% hint style="danger" %}
**Before executing this process, you must go through** [**the Operational Checklist**](/oc-europe-internal-doc/support-ops/operational-checklist-mandatory-before-action)**.**
{% endhint %}

This page outlines how we handle refund requests and payment disputes at Open Collective Europe.

These situations are rare but must be managed carefully to ensure transparency, donor trust, and compliance with both fiscal hosting and platform rules.

***

### Types of refund requests

We typically encounter four types of cases:

1. **Donor error**\
   A donor mistakenly sends a payment twice or to the wrong collective.
2. **Dispute via payment provider**\
   A donor opens a dispute via Stripe, PayPal, or their credit card.
3. **Internal error**\
   A transaction was incorrectly assigned, duplicated, or processed in error.
4. **Disagreement with how funds are used**\
   A donor disagrees with a project’s direction or spending but has no legal basis for a refund.

***

### General principles

* We are not legally required to refund donations, unless the payment was made in error or fraudulently.
* Refunds are granted **at OCE’s discretion**, unless the platform provider (Stripe, PayPal) forces a chargeback.
* Collectives should **never promise refunds to contributors**.

***

### Procedure: Refunds initiated by OCE

1. **Request received**\
   Via Freshdesk, email, or direct contact.
2. **Review and validation**
   * Confirm payment details (amount, method, date)
   * Confirm identity of requester
   * Check platform record for linked order
3. **Internal decision**
   * Jean-François or Ivan confirms whether refund should proceed
   * If the funds have already been used by the collective, refund may not be possible
4. **Refund processing**
   * Via Stripe: refund directly from the transaction interface
   * Via PayPal: issue refund and notify the donor
   * Manual bank transfer: only if absolutely necessary, with IBAN and documentation
5. **Recordkeeping**
   * Add notes in the platform (collective internal notes)
   * Save screenshots or email trail in Drive (`OCE – Admin – Refunds`)

***

### Procedure: Disputes from Stripe or PayPal

1. **Notification received**
   * Automatic notification from Stripe or PayPal
   * Freshdesk ticket or email alert
2. **Gather evidence**
   * Platform order ID
   * Donation date and collective page link
   * Any public or private communication with the donor
3. **Respond to provider**
   * Submit evidence through Stripe or PayPal dispute center
   * Indicate that it was a voluntary donation, non-refundable unless in error
4. **Outcome**
   * If dispute is lost, funds are withdrawn from OCE’s account
   * Document outcome in internal notes and Drive

***

### Communication templates

**Donor error example:**

> Thank you for reaching out. We’ve located your donation and can confirm the amount.\
> As this appears to have been submitted in error, we are happy to process a refund.\
> Please allow 5–7 business days for the funds to appear on your account.

**Non-refundable donation example:**

> Thank you for your message.\
> As per our terms and those of the platform, donations made to collectives are final and non-refundable unless clearly made in error.\
> We encourage you to reach out to the collective directly to share your concerns.

***

### Notes

* Never issue a refund without confirming the donor and transaction.
* In case of large or sensitive refunds, escalate to the Executive Director.
* Refunds that would endanger the collective’s ability to meet commitments should be refused.


# PayPal Disputes

This page documents the internal procedure for handling PayPal disputes related to donations or payments made to collectives hosted by Open Collective Europe.

PayPal disputes are rare, but when they occur, they are time-sensitive and must be handled carefully to protect OCE’s account and the integrity of our fiscal hosting model.

***

### What is a PayPal dispute?

A PayPal dispute is when a contributor files a claim to recover their funds, typically by marking a payment as:

* Unauthorized
* Not received
* Unsatisfactory or fraudulent

Even if the transaction is a donation, PayPal treats it as a potential commercial payment unless clearly labeled otherwise.

***

### When and how we are notified

* A dispute is opened directly via PayPal’s resolution center.
* The primary notification is sent to `oce@opencollective.com` and/or the finance contact.
* You typically have **10 calendar days** to respond.

***

### Step-by-step response process

#### 1. Locate the transaction

* Log in to the Open Collective platform and search by:
  * Donor email (if available)
  * Collective name
  * Donation amount
  * Date range
* Confirm that the transaction matches a legitimate donation

#### 2. Prepare evidence

Gather the following to support the legitimacy of the transaction:

* Screenshot of the donation tier or link on the Collective’s page
* Order ID on the platform (OC-EU-XXXXXXX)
* Date/time of transaction
* Public record of donation if visible on the platform
* Note that all donations are voluntary and clearly labeled as such

Save this information in Drive (under `OCE – Admin – Refunds & Disputes`)

#### 3. Respond to the dispute in PayPal

* Log in to PayPal
* Open the case and submit the evidence
* Use a clear explanation:

> "This payment was a voluntary donation made through a fundraising platform.\
